28: en autō gar zōmen kai kinoumentha kai esmen (en autō gar zōmen kai kinoumentha kai esmen): Paul quotes the Cretan poet Epimenides (in him we live and move and have our being). tou gar kai genos esmen: from Aratus's Phaenomena (for we are indeed his offspring). Paul's Areopagus speech uses pagan poets as partial witnesses to what the gospel discloses fully: universal human dependence on and kinship with the God who made from one (ex henos) all nations. The strategy is not syncretism but apologetic appropriation — common ground that the specific proclamation of resurrection then exceeds.
1: hōdoiporēsantes de tēn Amphipolin kai Apollōnian ('having passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia'): Luke's travel notation is characteristically specific — the Via Egnatia, the major Roman road through Macedonia, ran through these cities. The distance from Philippi to Thessalonica was about 100 miles. Luke does not report any mission activity in Amphipolis or Apollonia, implying either no significant synagogue or Gentile community, or a strategic focus on Thessalonica as the regional capital.
2: kata de to eiōthos tō Paulō eisēlthen pros autous ('as was Paul's custom, he went to them'): eiōthos (habitual custom) establishes synagogue-entry as Paul's standard operational method, not a one-time accommodation. Paul's synagogue-first pattern serves both theological principle (to the Jew first, Romans 1:16) and practical mission logic: Scriptural literacy, monotheism, and eschatological expectation created a receptive-or-rejecting audience in a defined venue. Epi sabbata tria ('for three Sabbaths') suggests a brief synagogue engagement before the broader Thessalonian mission.
3: dianoigōn kai paratithemenos hoti ton Christon edei pathein kai anastēnai ek nekrōn ('opening and demonstrating that it was necessary for the Messiah to suffer and rise from the dead'): the two-stage exegetical method — dianoigōn (opening the Scriptures, the same word as Luke 24:45) and paratithemenos (setting alongside, demonstrating by comparison) — is the apostolic interpretive technique. Edei (it was necessary) is Lukan divine necessity; the suffering and resurrection of the Messiah is not contingent but scripturally required. Paul then identifies Jesus as the one who fulfills these requirements.
4: tōn te sebomenōn Hellēnōn plēthos poly kai gynaikōn tōn prōtōn ouk oligai ('a great multitude of God-fearing Greeks and not a few prominent women'): Luke notes the social profile of the Thessalonian converts — a large Gentile contingent (the God-fearers) and socially prominent women. The prominent women's conversion replicates the pattern at Pisidian Antioch (Acts 13:50) and later at Berea (Acts 17:12), reflecting both the synagogue's attraction to elite Gentile women and Luke's consistent attention to women's role in the early mission.
5: zēlōsantes de hoi Ioudaioi ('the Jews being jealous'): the opposition pattern — Jewish leaders stirred by jealousy against Paul's mission success — recurs from 13:45 and will continue in Berea (Acts 17:13) and Corinth (Acts 18:12-17). anastatōsantes tēn polin ('having thrown the city into turmoil'): anastatōsantes will be used of Paul himself in 21:38 as a charge; here it is the accusers who create the disturbance. The charge is projection.
6: hoi tēn oikoumenēn anastatōsantes ('those who have turned the world upside down'): the charge is simultaneously the accusers' indictment and Luke's clearest description of the gospel's social impact. Anastatoō (overturn, unsettle, throw into confusion) was used of military and social upheaval; the crowd's accusation is that Paul's movement is destabilizing the imperial order. For Luke's reader, the charge is true in the deepest sense — the gospel does overturn the world's structures — but false as a political crime: the kingdom of God advances by proclamation, not revolt.
7: houtos pantes apenanti tōn dogmatōn Kaisaros prattousin basilea heteron legontes einai Iēsoun ('these all act against Caesar's decrees, saying there is another king, Jesus'): the political charge repurposes an actual apostolic claim. The missionaries did proclaim Jesus as kyrios (Lord) and king; in the Roman world, this is a political challenge to Caesar's title. Luke records the charge not to deny it but to show that it is misunderstood: the kingdom Jesus inaugurates does not compete with Rome on Rome's terms but transcends it entirely.
8: etaraxan de ton ochlon kai tous politarchas akouontas tauta ('they threw the crowd and the city officials into turmoil when they heard these things'): politarchas ('city officials') is a Macedonian civic title attested in inscriptions from Thessalonica — one of many Lukan place-name and title details confirmed by archaeology, supporting his historical reliability. The turmoil (etaraxan) is the crowd's and officials' reaction to the politically charged accusation, not to actual violence by the missionaries.
9: labontes to hikanon para tou Iasonos kai tōn loipōn apelysan autous ('having taken security from Jason and the others, they released them'): to hikanon ('sufficient/bond') is the legal term for bail or security deposit — a financial guarantee of good behavior. Jason's house had hosted the missionaries; he takes legal responsibility for the community's conduct. The mechanism is Roman legal process being used to manage a religious dispute — the kind of cross-category entanglement that characterizes Acts throughout.
10: hoi de adelphoi eutheos dia nyktos exepemyan ton te Paulon kai ton Silan eis Berroian ('the brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas away to Berea by night'): the nighttime departure is a security measure against continued mob action. Berea (modern Veria) is about 50 miles southwest of Thessalonica — on a different road from the Via Egnatia and less exposed to Thessalonian influence. The brothers' initiative in arranging Paul's departure shows the community protecting the apostle while continuing their own witness in the city.
11: eugenesteroi tōn en Thessalonikē ('more noble-minded than those in Thessalonica'): eugenēs (noble/well-born) here describes intellectual openness and diligence rather than social class. anakrinō ('examining/investigating'): the judicial-interrogation word — used in Acts for official investigations (Acts 4:9; Acts 12:19; Acts 24:8; Acts 25:26; Acts 28:18) — is applied to Scripture study. The Bereans subjected Paul's claims to the same rigorous testing as a court would examine testimony. Luke presents this not as distrust but as exemplary biblical practice: testing apostolic claims against the text.
12: kai tōn Hellēnidōn gynaikōn tōn euschēmonōn kai andrōn ouk oligoi ('and not a few prominent Greek women and men'): the social profile again notes prominent women first. The Berean community seems weighted toward educated Hellenistic Gentiles who found the Scripture-based reasoning persuasive. This profile anticipates the Areopagus audience (Acts 17:18-21) where educated philosophical discourse will meet the gospel.
13: saleuontes kai tarassontes tous ochlous ('shaking and stirring up the crowds'): the Thessalonian Jews follow Paul to Berea specifically to disrupt his mission there. Saleuō (shake) and tarassō (stir up) both describe deliberate agitation — not natural response but organized opposition. The pattern of Jewish opponents from one city following Paul to another will continue (cf. Acts 20:3; Acts 21:27). Luke records it without editorial comment; the opposition intensifies as the mission advances.
14: eutheos de tote ton Paulon exapesteilan hoi adelphoi poreuesthai hōs epi tēn thalassan ('the brothers immediately sent Paul away to go toward the sea'): Paul is separated from Silas and Timothy for strategic reasons — the opposition is directed personally at Paul. The community again protects the apostle by arranging his departure. Hōs epi tēn thalassan ('as far as the sea') may be misdirection — the phrase could mean toward the coast rather than definitively by sea — keeping Paul's exact route uncertain to his pursuers.
15: kathiston auton heōs Athēnōn ('they brought him as far as Athens'): the escorts' mission is complete when Paul reaches Athens. His instructions to send Silas and Timothy quickly (hōs tachista) reflects his awareness that he needs his co-workers to continue the Thessalonian mission. Paul's presence in Athens — the intellectual capital of the ancient world — is the result not of strategic planning but of persecution-driven movement. The Areopagus speech will not have been on Paul's itinerary.
16: parōxyneto to pneuma autou en autō ('his spirit was provoked within him'): paroxynō is the LXX term for God's anger at idolatry (Deuteronomy 9:18; Psalms 78:58; Isaiah 65:3). Paul's response to Athens is not tourist admiration but the same moral-theological reaction that the prophets had to Israel's idolatry. Kateidōlon ousan tēn polin ('the city being full of idols'): Athens's religious density was proverbial in antiquity; Petronius commented that it was easier to find a god than a man there. Paul's grief mirrors God's own grief at the sight.
17: men oun en tē synagōgē dielegeto tois Ioudaiois kai tois sebomenois ('so in the synagogue he reasoned with the Jews and God-fearers'): Paul maintains his synagogue-first pattern even in Athens, where the main encounter will be with philosophers. Kai en tē agora kata pasan hēmeran ('and in the market-place every day'): the agora (marketplace) was the center of Athenian intellectual and civic life — where Socrates had conversed. Paul's daily engagement in the agora replicates the Socratic method of street-level philosophical dialogue.
18: spermologos ('seed-picker/babbler'): a term of contempt used for birds that pick up scattered seeds, then applied to people who pick up fragments of others' ideas without understanding them. The Epicureans (followers of Epicurus — pleasure-through-absence-of-pain, no divine providence, atomic materialism) and Stoics (rational divine principle permeating all things, virtue as the highest good) represent the two dominant Hellenistic philosophical schools. Ti an theloi ho spermologos houtos legein ('what would this seed-picker want to say?') is the philosophically condescending response to Paul's street preaching.
19: Areiou Pagou ('the Areopagus'): the ancient court on the hill northwest of the Acropolis, traditionally associated with judgment (mythology: Ares was tried there for murder). By Paul's day it functioned as an advisory council overseeing education, religious practices, and public morality in Athens. The invitation to speak there is a formal hearing, not merely a philosophical seminar — Paul is being evaluated as a new religious teacher. Xenē daimonia ('foreign divinities'): technically introducing new gods was a prosecutable offense in Athens (Socrates was convicted of it); the Areopagus appearance is a quasi-legal proceeding.
20: xenizonta gar tina eispherei eis tas akoais hēmōn ('for you are bringing some strange things to our hearing'): xenizō (to seem foreign/strange) is the Athenians' reaction to anastasis (resurrection). To Greek ears, resurrection was strange — not merely unfamiliar but conceptually incoherent within materialist or dualist frameworks. The Stoics believed in a cosmic recycling of souls; the Epicureans denied any afterlife; neither had conceptual categories for resurrection of bodies. Paul's proclamation genuinely 'sounds foreign' in the philosophers' ears.
21: Athēnaioi de pantes kai hoi epidēmountes xenoi eis ouden heteron ēukairoun e legein ti e akouein ti kainoteron ('all Athenians and the foreigners who lived there had time for nothing other than telling or hearing the very latest thing'): Luke's aside is his most explicitly satirical observation in Acts. The characterization of Athenians as addicted to intellectual novelty was a classical cliché (Demosthenes, Thucydides) — Luke's irony is gentle but pointed: the people who prided themselves on intellectual sophistication were consumed by the appetite for the new, making them simultaneously receptive to Paul's message and likely to dismiss it for the next novelty.
22: deisidaimonesterous hymas theōrō ('I observe that you are very religious/superstitious'): deisidaimōn is deliberately ambiguous — it can mean 'very religious' (compliment) or 'very superstitious' (criticism). Paul's opening honors Athenian piety while leaving room for the critique to follow. The rhetorical strategy is classical captatio benevolentiae — winning the audience's goodwill before making challenging claims. Paul adapts his rhetorical approach to his audience without compromising his message.
23: AGNŌSTŌ THEŌ ('to an unknown god'): archaeological evidence confirms the existence of altars to unknown gods in Athens and elsewhere; Pausanias (2nd century AD) mentions them. Paul's use of this altar as a point of contact is a masterpiece of apologetic method: he does not begin with Jewish Scripture (inappropriate for a non-Jewish audience) but with a void in their existing religious framework. The unknown God they have acknowledged by ignorance is the God he is about to make known.
24: ho theos ho poiēsas ton kosmon kai panta ta en autō ('the God who made the world and everything in it'): Paul begins with Creator-theology — the ground shared with OT tradition and partially with Stoicism. Kyrios ouranou kai gēs hyparchōn ('being Lord of heaven and earth'): the title echoes Acts 4:24 (the community's prayer) and OT creation-theology (Genesis 14:19; Psalms 146:6). Ouk en cheiropoiētois naois katoikei ('does not live in temples made by human hands'): citing Isaiah 66:1-2 and 1 Kings 8:27 without citation — the transcendence of God over cultic structures, recognizable to any Stoic as well.
25: oude hypo cheirōn anthrōpinōn therapeudo prosdeomenos tinos ('nor is he served by human hands as if he needed anything'): the self-sufficiency of God (aseitas) is simultaneously OT theology (Psalms 50:12-14) and Stoic theism. The Epicurean gods were famously self-sufficient and uninvolved; Paul's God is self-sufficient but supremely involved, being the source of all life. Autos didous pāsin zōēn kai pnoēn kai ta panta ('he himself gives to all life and breath and all things'): the life-giving Creator is the antithesis of the idol who receives.
26: ex henos epoiēsen pan ethnos anthrōpōn ('from one he made every nation of mankind'): ex henos (from one) — the one ancestor, Adam — establishes the unity of the human race against Athenian claims of autochthony (being born from their own soil, making them superior to other peoples). Paul's universalism has OT ground (Gen 1-3; Deuteronomy 32:8) and challenges Athenian ethnic pride. Horothesias tēs katoikias autōn ('the boundaries of their dwelling places'): divine sovereignty over national territories and times — the same sovereignty Paul named in Acts 13:17.
27: zētein ton theon, ei ara ge psēlaphēseian auton kai heuroien ('to seek God, if perhaps they might feel/grope after him and find him'): psēlaphōō ('to feel/grope') is used for blind groping in the dark (LXX Deuteronomy 28:29; Job 5:14) and for Thomas's tactile examination of the risen Christ (John 20:27). Humanity's natural religious search is described as groping in partial darkness — not futile, since God has structured history toward finding, but not self-sufficient, since the proclamation of Acts 17:30-31 is required for the search to conclude. Kai ge ou makran apo henos hekastou hēmōn hyparchonta ('though he is not far from each one of us') — the proximity language resonates with Stoic immanence.
29: ouk opheilomen nomizein chrysō e argyrō e lithō, charagmati technēs kai enthymēseōs anthrōpou, to theion einai homoion ('we ought not to think that the divine being is like gold or silver or stone, an image formed by human art and imagination'): Paul here deploys the OT idol polemic (Isaiah 44:9-20; Psalms 115:4-8) in philosophical language accessible to Stoics (who also rejected idolatry conceptually). Charagmati technēs kai enthymēseōs ('image formed by craft and human imagination'): the Stoic position that the divine cannot be captured in material form aligns with Paul's argument, though for different reasons.
30: tous men oun chronous tēs agnoias hyperidōn ho theos ('the times of ignorance God overlooked'): hypereidon (overlooked/passed over) is a deliberate echo of Romans 3:25-26, where God 'passed over' sins in the time before Christ's atonement. The 'times of ignorance' (chronous tēs agnoias) correspond to what Paul calls 'the present time' preceding the full revelation of righteousness. Nyn de paraggellei tois anthrōpois pantas pantachou metanoein ('but now he commands all people everywhere to repent'): the eschatological 'now' — the new era of accountability that Christ's resurrection inaugurates.
31: en andri hō hōrisen ('by a man whom he appointed'): the universal judgment is mediated through a specific historical person — the particular-universal structure of the gospel's scandal. Pistin parescen pasin anastesas auton ek nekrōn ('he has given proof to all by raising him from the dead'): the resurrection is the public verification — pistis here means evidence/proof (the forensic sense, not the trust-sense), the publicly demonstrable credential that the appointed judge has been vindicated. The speech ends where all apostolic speeches end: the resurrection as the undeniable divine act requiring response.
32: anastasis nekrōn ('resurrection of the dead'): the philosophical stumbling block for Greeks. Stoics believed in a periodic cosmic recycling of souls; Epicureans denied any afterlife; both rejected the bodily resurrection as conceptually incoherent within their frameworks. Hoi men echleuazon ('some mocked'): chleuazō is open mockery — not polite skepticism but contemptuous dismissal. The divided response — mockery, deferral, belief — mirrors the range of responses throughout Acts. The gospel divides because its central claim (resurrection) is simultaneously the most incredible and the most decisive truth it asserts.
33: Paul's departure ek mesou autōn ('from their midst') is neither triumphant nor despairing; Luke narrates it as a simple withdrawal from a mixed-response audience. The Areopagus speech achieves no visible mass conversion — in contrast to Pentecost (3,000) or Pisidian Antioch. Luke records it without apology or explanation; the philosophical audience's response to the resurrection is what it is. Paul's self-reflection on his Corinthian approach (1 Corinthians 2:1-5) may reflect on the Athens experience.
34: Dionysios ho Areopagitēs kai gynē onomati Damaris kai heteroi syn autois ('Dionysius the Areopagite and a woman named Damaris and others with them'): the named converts from the Areopagus are few but not negligible. Dionysius (Areopagitēs) was a member of the Areopagus council — a civic elite convert. Damaris is otherwise unknown; her presence in the named converts alongside a council member suggests she was also a person of some social standing. Luke's naming of converts throughout Acts is consistent with his access to community tradition that remembered the first members.
